content.text: Qualitative readiness judgments ('feels stable', 'looks good') do not scale and cannot be audited. Quantitative launch criteria replace judgment calls with measurable thresholds: specific numbers that the system must meet before a launch proceeds, checked automatically, with a clear severity classification that determines whether a miss is a hard block or a manageable risk.
A launch criterion without a number is an opinion. A number without a measurement method is aspirational. A measurement without a severity classification is unactionable.

This sub-subtopic covers the full framework: how to define criteria, which categories to cover, how to set thresholds from baseline data, severity classification, automated checking, and the YAML schema that ties criteria to the Go/No-Go decision gate.

attrs.alt: Quantitative launch criteria categories and severity levels

content.text: Launch criteria for Claude API features fall into five categories. Each covers a different failure mode that the others do not catch. Omitting any category creates a blind spot.
Response time degradation that affects UX
Insufficient test coverage for new code paths
Early signal of systemic issues in production traffic
CPU, memory, connection pool at expected peak

A threshold set without baseline data will be wrong — either too lenient (passes bad launches) or too strict (blocks good ones). The standard approach is to measure the metric over a stable reference window and set the threshold as a multiple or percentile of that baseline.
Use 7–14 days of production data, excluding known incident periods.
For new services with no production history, use load-test data as the baseline with a wider margin (1.5× instead of 1.2×).
Re-baseline after any architectural change that legitimately shifts the metric — don't carry an outdated baseline that makes stable launches look like violations.

Thresholds are stored in the launch document as a structured YAML block. This makes them machine-readable (the CI gate reads them directly), version-controlled (thresholds are part of the launch record), and auditable (the Go/No-Go decision references specific numbers, not verbal agreements).

# In docs/launches/{service}-{version}.yaml
launch_criteria:
  # --- Error Rate ---
  error_rate:
    description: "HTTP 5xx rate on the API endpoint"
    measurement:
      query: 'sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="triage-api",status=~"5.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="triage-api"}[5m]))'
      window_minutes: 10
    baseline: 0.00031          # 0.031% — 95th pct of 14-day window
    thresholds:
      p0: 0.00093              # 3× baseline — hard No-Go
      p1: 0.000465             # 1.5× baseline — conditional Go, DRI must accept
      p2: 0.000341             # 1.1× baseline — warning, proceed with monitoring
    current_value: null        # filled in by CI gate

  # --- p99 Latency ---
  latency_p99:
    description: "99th percentile API response latency (seconds)"
    measurement:
      query: 'histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{service="triage-api"}[5m]))'
      window_minutes: 10
    sla_seconds: 0.5           # contractual SLA
    baseline: 0.21             # 95th pct of 14-day window
    thresholds:
      p0: 1.0                  # 2× SLA — hard No-Go
      p1: 0.625                # 1.25× SLA — conditional
      p2: 0.525                # 1.05× SLA — warning
    current_value: null

  # --- Eval Score ---
  eval_score:
    description: "Composite eval score from automated harness (0.0–1.0)"
    measurement:
      tool: "evals/run_launch_evals.py"
      suite: "launch-eval-suite-v3"
    baseline: 0.971            # score on reference prompt set
    thresholds:
      p0: 0.922                # < 95% of baseline — hard No-Go
      p1: 0.951                # 95–98% of baseline — conditional
      p2: 0.961                # 98–99% of baseline — warning
    current_value: null
    higher_is_better: true

  # --- Safety Compliance Rate ---
  safety_compliance:
    description: "Fraction of policy-test prompts with compliant responses"
    measurement:
      tool: "safety/run_policy_check.py"
      test_set: "policy-test-set-v2"
    baseline: 0.9994           # established on approved model version
    thresholds:
      p0: 0.995                # < 99.5% — hard No-Go, safety block
      p1: 0.997                # 99.5–99.7% — conditional
      p2: 0.9990               # 99.7–99.94% — warning
    current_value: null
    higher_is_better: true

  # --- Test Coverage ---
  test_coverage:
    description: "Line coverage for new code paths in this release"
    measurement:
      tool: "pytest --cov --cov-report=json"
      scope: "diff-only"       # coverage only on changed lines
    thresholds:
      p0: 0.80                 # < 80% — hard No-Go
      p1: 0.90                 # 80–90% — conditional
      p2: 0.95                 # 90–95% — warning
    current_value: null
    higher_is_better: true

  # --- Canary Health ---
  canary:
    description: "No critical alerts fired during canary window"
    measurement:
      window_minutes: 30
      traffic_percent: 5
    thresholds:
      p0: "any_critical_alert"
      p1: "degraded_no_alert"
      p2: "elevated_within_sla"
    current_value: null

The CI pipeline reads the launch criteria YAML, runs the measurements, populates the current values, and exits with the appropriate code. Any P0 violation fails the pipeline. P1 violations require a recorded DRI risk acceptance. P2 violations are logged as warnings but do not block.
Threshold Calibration for Different Launch Types

The same absolute threshold does not apply uniformly — a 0.5% error rate may be acceptable for an experimental internal API but catastrophic for a production payment endpoint. Calibrate thresholds by launch type:
Real users but explicit opt-in; rollback faster
Existing expectations; breakage is a regression
No prior baseline from users; more margin
Safety score is independent hard block at all tiers
Highest scrutiny; any regression is critical

# In docs/launches/{service}-{version}.yaml
launch_type: "ga_existing_users"    # internal | beta | ga_new | ga_existing | safety | breaking

# The CI gate reads launch_type and applies the corresponding multipliers.
# Override individual thresholds below to tighten further if needed.
# You cannot loosen thresholds below the launch_type floor without DRI + safety sign-off.

# launch_type multipliers (applied if individual thresholds not set)
# See scripts/launch_type_thresholds.yaml for the full table
Handling Missing or Invalid Baseline Data
A threshold derived from bad baseline data is worse than no threshold — it gives false confidence. The measurement pipeline must validate the baseline before computing thresholds.
Minimum sample count: at least 1,000 data points per metric over the baseline window. Reject baselines with fewer samples.
Stability check: coefficient of variation (stddev / mean) must be < 0.5. A highly variable baseline produces unstable thresholds.
Recency: baseline data must not be older than 30 days. Older data may not reflect the current architecture.
No-incident filter: automatically exclude time windows flagged as incidents (via PagerDuty or status page events) from the baseline calculation.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/validate_baseline.py
import statistics, sys

def validate_baseline(values, metric_name, min_samples=1000, max_cv=0.5):
    if len(values) < min_samples:
        raise ValueError(
            f"{metric_name}: only {len(values)} samples (need {min_samples}). "
            "Extend the baseline window or use load-test data."
        )
    mean = statistics.mean(values)
    if mean == 0:
        raise ValueError(f"{metric_name}: mean is 0 — metric may not be instrumented")
    cv = statistics.stdev(values) / mean
    if cv > max_cv:
        raise ValueError(
            f"{metric_name}: coefficient of variation {cv:.2f} exceeds {max_cv}. "
            "Baseline is too unstable. Investigate metric before setting thresholds."
        )
    print(f"  [{metric_name}] baseline OK: n={len(values)}, mean={mean:.5f}, cv={cv:.2f}")
    return mean

# Example usage
import random
# Simulate 2 weeks of 5-min samples (2016 points) with realistic variation
error_samples = [max(0, random.gauss(0.0003, 0.00005)) for _ in range(2016)]
try:
    mean = validate_baseline(error_samples, "error_rate")
    print(f"  Recommended P0 threshold: {mean * 3:.5f}")
    print(f"  Recommended P1 threshold: {mean * 1.5:.5f}")
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"BASELINE VALIDATION FAILED: {e}")
    sys.exit(1)

Thresholds set once and never revisited become incorrect as the system evolves. A service that was handling 100 RPS when thresholds were set will have a different error floor at 10,000 RPS. Schedule threshold reviews as part of the quarterly production readiness process.
Setting P0 at absolute value, not relative to baseline
Passes bad launches after a system degrades; blocks good launches after optimization
Always express thresholds as multiples of the current baseline
Using mean instead of 95th percentile for baseline
Outliers inflate the mean; threshold is set too high
Use p95 or p99 of the baseline distribution
Same thresholds for all launch types
Internal tests block on production thresholds; GA launches pass on internal-tier thresholds
Apply launch_type multiplier table; tighten individually if needed
Safety score not a separate hard block
A degraded safety score is accepted by DRI as a P1 risk
Safety compliance is always a hard block; DRI cannot override it
Thresholds not updated after architecture change
Stored baseline is stale; thresholds are wrong
Trigger re-baseline whenever infrastructure changes affect the metric floor
Canary not measured before full rollout
P0 conditions not caught until 100% traffic is affected
Canary gate always precedes any traffic shift above 5%

Quantitative launch criteria convert 'is this ready?' from a judgment call into a measurement problem. The framework here — baseline-derived thresholds, three-tier severity classification, automated CI gate, and launch-type multipliers — ensures that every launch is evaluated against consistent, auditable, and calibrated numbers.
Cover all five categories: error rate, latency, eval/quality, safety compliance, test coverage, and canary health.
Set thresholds as multiples of the measured baseline (p95 over 7–14 days), not as arbitrary absolute values.
Classify violations as P0 (hard block), P1 (DRI risk accept required), or P2 (warning, proceed with monitoring).
Store thresholds in the launch YAML so CI can gate on them and the Go/No-Go forum can reference specific numbers.
Safety compliance is always a hard block — it cannot be accepted by DRI override at any severity level.
Review and re-baseline thresholds quarterly or after any architectural change that shifts the metric floor.
